text: The surgeon uses five trocars positioned around the patient's abdomen. Positioned to the right of the patient, the surgeon employs a uterine hanging technique with a straight needle suture to expose the pelvic area.

The surgeon begins the dissection of the mesocolon from medial to lateral, reaching and dividing the inferior mesenteric artery between clips while preserving the superior hypogastric plexus. Using mesocolic retroperitoneal interfaces as landmarks, retroperitoneal structures are preserved during the dissection, which continues until the splenic angle is reached. The inferior mesenteric vein is dissected, ligated, and divided near its origin, facilitating greater mobility of the colon.
The surgeon performs lateral mobilization of the colon, facilitated by prior medial dissection. The splenic flexure is mobilized, followed by transverse colon mobilization.

The surgeon begins mesorectal dissection at the posterior wall, taking care to preserve the inferior hypogastric plexus. The dissection progresses to reveal the pivotal rectalis muscles, followed by the initiation of right lateral mesorectal dissection.
The surgeon performs deeper mesorectal dissection in a circumferential manner, utilizing counter-attraction techniques from side to side to reach the proveractolous muscles in each quadrant.

The surgeon uses an anal retractor system to perform intersphincteric dissection approximately one centimeter distal to the anal mucosa in a circumferential manner with a fine needle electrocautery. After completing the dissection at both sphincters, a purse-string suture is applied to close the distal stump.
The specimen is extracted through a transanal pull-through technique under laparoscopic view, showing no tension, torsion, or inadequate perfusion from both internal and external perspectives.

The surgeon highlights the absence of tension due to extensive column mobilization. The specimen section line is dissected using a hybrid energy device, trimming the specimen to leave approximately four centimeters of colonic tissue. Four simple stitches are placed at cardinal points to prevent retraction of the tissue. The permeability of the specimen is checked, and microscopically, it shows a total mesorectal excision without disruption of the mesorectal fascia.
